short.headline: India is online now. The connection is mobile-first and unequal.
short.dek: Internet use reached 70% in 2025. Cheap mobile data, UPI, and wireless networks changed daily life, but gender and rural gaps remain.
short.body: India is now a connected country, but mostly through the phone. Internet use rose from 0% in 1990 to 70% in 2025. Electricity access reached 99.5% by 2023, mobile subscriptions were 79.4 per 100 people in 2024, and mobile data use climbed from 0.05 GB per subscriber per month in 2013 to 23.85 GB in 2025. The price story is just as sharp: mobile data fell from Rs 200 per GB in 2016 to Rs 8.27 in 2025. UPI then turned connectivity into daily payment infrastructure, with 22.35 billion monthly transactions in April 2026. But the average hides the divide: 76.7% of men used the internet in 2025, compared with 63.1% of women.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this mean for a normal Indian, macha?
macha.body: Think about it: 30 years ago, almost nobody in India had the internet. Today, 7 out of 10 people do. That's a massive shift. It happened because we got electricity everywhere, and then mobile data became unbelievably cheap. Remember when ₹200 for a GB was normal? Now it's ₹8. So we are streaming, paying with UPI, and using our phones for everything. But don't get too excited: the 70% is an average. If you are a woman in a village, your chances of being online are much lower. And compared to China, we still have a long way to go. Basically, India is now a connected country, but the connection is not equal for everyone.
macha.soWhat: The internet is everywhere in India, but who gets to use it still depends on your gender and where you live.

article.title: How connected is India?
article.standfirst: India went online through cheap mobile data, not home broadband. That made the internet common, but not equal.
article.bodyMarkdown:
India is connected now. That is the big answer. But the shape of that connection matters.

This is not a story of every home getting a fast fixed broadband line and a laptop. India's route was more improvised and more Indian: a phone, a SIM, cheap data, a charging point, and then UPI at the shop counter. That route gave the country speed and scale. It also left real gaps in quality, gender access, and rural access.

## How many Indians are online now?

The headline number is 70%. That is the share of India's population using the internet in 2025 in the local OWID series. In 1990, the same series was at zero. In one generation, the internet moved from elite infrastructure to ordinary infrastructure.

This number should be read carefully. It counts people using the internet, not the number of SIM cards, phones, or broadband lines. It also does not tell us whether someone has fast internet, daily access, privacy, confidence, or control over the device. Still, 70% is a large shift. It means the internet is now part of the country's everyday operating system.

## What had to come before the internet boom?

The hidden precondition was electricity. In 1993, only 50.9% of Indians had access to electricity. By 2023, the figure was 99.5%. That does not mean every household gets reliable power all day. It means the basic condition for a phone-first internet became nearly universal.

Digital access is physical before it is digital. Phones need charging. Towers need power. Shops need working devices. Schools and clinics need electricity before software matters. The connection story sits on top of the electricity story.

## Why did mobile beat fixed broadband?

India's connection story is mobile-first. In 2024, there were 79.4 mobile cellular subscriptions per 100 people. Fixed broadband was only 3.2 per 100 people. Fixed telephones were 2.7 per 100 people.

That comparison explains the whole route. India did not wait for wires to reach every home. It jumped to mobile networks. That made access cheaper and faster to spread. It also means many people's internet is still a small-screen, prepaid, mobile-data experience.

## How much data do Indians use now?

The data-use chart is where the behavioural shift becomes visible. In December 2013, the average mobile subscriber used only 0.05 GB per month. By September 2025, that had risen to 23.85 GB.

That change is not only technical. It explains why video, messaging, maps, forms, delivery work, classes, and entertainment all moved onto the phone. A country does not feel online because a network exists somewhere. It feels online when people use enough data for the internet to become habit.

## What happened to the price of data?

The price chart moves in the opposite direction. In June 2016, one GB of mobile data cost Rs 200 in the local IndiaDataHub series. By September 2025, it cost Rs 8.27.

The reader does not need a technical model to understand the pattern. Data became far cheaper, and people used far more of it. Cheap data changed what people expected a phone to do. It also changed the business pressure on telecom companies, which is a separate story this page does not yet cover.

## How did connectivity change payments?

UPI is the clearest daily-life proof that connection became behaviour. In April 2020, UPI processed about 1 billion monthly transactions. By April 2026, it processed 22.35 billion.

This chart is not only about finance. It is about trust, habit, and infrastructure. A small vendor can accept a QR payment. A customer can pay without cash. A family can move money instantly. A phone connection becomes a money rail.

The caveat matters. Transaction count is not transaction value. A Rs 10 payment and a Rs 10,000 payment both count once. Cash has not disappeared.

## Is India's internet mostly wireless?

Yes. By September 2025, wireless internet subscribers were 973.39 million. Fixed-line internet subscribers were 44.42 million. That is the clearest infrastructure split in the article.

Wireless scale is India's advantage. It reaches faster than fibre. It lowers the entry cost. It lets the internet travel with the person. But wireless also carries the quality problem. Speeds can fall when networks are crowded. Coverage can break. A family can have a subscription but still lack a stable learning or working connection.

## Where is the rural-urban divide?

In April 2026, urban India had 783.12 million telecom subscribers. Rural India had 554.41 million. These numbers now come directly from TRAI monthly report PDFs extracted and validated in the local data pipeline. The rural line has grown strongly over the longer IndiaDataHub history, but the official monthly PDF series gives us the latest high-frequency view.

Rural India has a large population, so totals can look better than per-person access. This chart tells us where connections are counted. It does not tell us whether the connection is fast, reliable, private, or enough for work and study.

## How far does the phone network reach?

TRAI's April 2026 report puts total tele-density at 84.76. In plain English, tele-density is telephone connections compared with population. It is useful because subscriber totals alone can mislead: a country can add connections while the population denominator also changes.

But this number needs discipline. Tele-density is not the share of people with phones. One person can hold more than one SIM, and some connections are not personal phones. TRAI's recent reports also separate M2M cellular connections, so this chart is best read as a rough reach indicator, not a clean count of connected human beings.

## Are people switching networks?

In April 2026, TRAI recorded 14.74 million Mobile Number Portability requests. MNP lets a subscriber try to keep the same number while moving to another operator. It is a small but useful behaviour signal: people are not only connected, they can also push back when price, coverage, or service disappoints them.

The caveat is simple. A request is not the same as a completed switch, and it is not a count of unique people. Still, monthly MNP requests belong in this story because a connected country is also a market where users can move.

## What is the gender gap online?

In 2025, 76.7% of Indian men used the internet, compared with 63.1% of women. Both numbers have improved sharply since 2018, when the figures were 25% for men and 14.9% for women. But the gap has not vanished.

A household can be connected while women inside it are less connected. Device ownership, permission, safety, language, money, and confidence can all sit between a network and a person. The chart proves the gap. It does not prove every cause.

## How does India compare with the world?

India's 70% internet use in 2025 sits slightly below the world average of 73.6% and far below China's 91.6%. There is no comparable 2025 figure for the United States, so the chart leaves it out rather than guessing.

The right reading is neither triumph nor shame. India moved fast from almost no internet to majority internet use. It also remains behind countries where internet use is closer to universal. For India, the next battle is not only adding users. It is improving the quality and equality of access.

So the answer is this: India is connected, and the phone made it happen. Cheap data made the connection ordinary. UPI made it useful every day. But a connected country can still be unequal. The next question is who gets a good connection, who controls it, and who is still outside it.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: The data shows how India went from almost no internet users in 1990 to 70% in 2025, driven by electrification, cheap mobile data, and a mobile-first infrastructure. But big disparities remain by gender and geography.

selectedDataPoints.label: Individuals using the internet: Share of the population using the Internet
selectedDataPoints.reason: The headline number: 70% in 2025, from 0% in 1990.

selectedDataPoints.label: Fixed broadband subscriptions, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: 3.15 per 100, very low compared to mobile.

selectedDataPoints.label: Mobile data used per subscriber per month, latest
Mobile data used per subscriber per month, earliest
Tele-density, total, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Adds a population-relative reach measure from official TRAI PDFs.

selectedDataPoints.label: Mobile Number Portability requests, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Shows switching pressure and user behaviour inside the connected telecom market.

pullQuotes.numberLabel: Individuals using the internet: Share of the population using the Internet

pullQuotes.quote: Mobile data cost collapsed from ₹200 to ₹8 per GB.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of it as the percentage of people in a country who have gone online in the last three months. It doesn't count how many devices or SIM cards you have. It counts actual people. So if 70% is the number, that means 7 out of every 10 Indians used the internet recently. It's not about having a smartphone; it's about using it.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This is the main measure the government and international agencies use to know how 'connected' a country is.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The amount of internet data you consume on your phone. Measured in gigabytes (GB). One GB is roughly enough to watch an hour of video on YouTube. So 23.85 GB a month means the average subscriber uses enough data to stream about 24 hours of video.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's mobile data usage is among the highest in the world. It shows how central the phone is for entertainment, work, and payments.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A system that lets you send money from your bank account to someone else's bank account instantly using a phone app. Think of it as cash, but digital. You don't need a credit card or a debit card. Just a phone number and a UPI app like Google Pay or PhonePe.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: UPI has become the most common way Indians pay for everything from chai to groceries. The explosive growth shows how connectivity has changed daily life.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Fixed broadband is a wire that enters your home, like fiber optic or cable. It is usually faster and more reliable, but needs a physical connection. Wireless is internet through mobile towers, it goes to your phone. In India, most people use wireless because it's cheaper and easier to set up.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's internet is overwhelmingly wireless. That means coverage can be spotty in remote areas, and speeds may be lower than what fixed broadband offers.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The gap in internet access and use between people living in villages (rural) and those living in cities (urban). City dwellers usually have more reliable and faster connections because infrastructure is better.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Although rural subscribers are growing fast, urban areas still have many more connections. This means someone in a village is less likely to have the same quality of internet as someone in a city.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The difference between men and women in how much they use the internet. In many countries, including India, fewer women are online than men. This can be due to social norms, cost, lack of access to smartphones, or education.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's gender gap is 13.6 percentage points: men (76.7%) vs women (63.1%). It means a significant portion of the population, mostly women, is being left out of the digital economy.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Shows the long-term trend clearly on mobile
Connects prerequisite to internet growth
Allows comparison of three series on one chart
Shows the post-Jio spike with sufficient granularity
Mirror of data usage; quarterly shows the decline clearly
Monthly frequency captures rapid growth
Shows dominance of wireless over time
Monthly shows convergence trend

visualizationDecisions.bestChartType: latest bars (or small multiples for trend)
visualizationDecisions.reason: Easy to compare two bars for men and women

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: India vs China vs world internet use
visualizationDecisions.reason: Simple comparison across entities
Shows network reach relative to population, with a strong caveat about connections not people
Adds a behavioural telecom-market signal that is directly available in TRAI PDFs

chartExplainers.title: Indians online, % of population
chartExplainers.takeaway: India went from 0% internet users in 1990 to 70% in 2025, a digital revolution in one generation.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the share of Indians who used the internet in the previous three months. In 1990, it was 0%. For decades it crept up slowly, 12.3% in 2013, 20% in 2018. Then the curve steepened sharply, hitting 70% by 2025. The main driver was the arrival of cheap mobile data and smartphones. Each point on the line is the percentage of all people, not just adults. So 70% means 7 out of 10 Indians, including children and the elderly, are online. The data is from OWID, which combines survey data and ITU estimates.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the steep rise after 2015; each number is the share of total population that used the internet.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse with 'subscribers', this counts actual users, not connections.

chartExplainers.title: The foundation: access to electricity
chartExplainers.takeaway: Electricity access rose from 50.9% in 1993 to 99.5% in 2023, enabling the internet boom.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the percentage of Indians with access to electricity. In 1993, just over half the population had power. The line climbs gradually, crossing 90% around 2015 and reaching 99.5% by 2023. Without power, you cannot charge a phone or run a tower. So this rise was essential. The internet boom would not have happened without near-universal electrification. The two trends, electricity and internet, rise together. Data from World Bank.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Simple line from low to near 100%; watch for the long, slow rise before acceleration.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume reliable supply, access does not mean 24/7 electricity.

chartExplainers.title: How India connects: mobile vs broadband vs landline
chartExplainers.takeaway: Mobile subscriptions (79.35 per 100 people) dwarf broadband (3.15) and landlines (2.7), India is mobile-first.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart compares three types of communication subscriptions per 100 people. The mobile line starts from zero in 1960 and climbs steeply, reaching 79.35 in 2024. The fixed broadband line barely leaves the bottom: it went from 0 in 2001 to 3.15 in 2024. The fixed telephone line peaked long ago and declined to 2.7. India skipped the era of wired connections and went straight to mobile. But a 'subscription' is a SIM card, not a person. Many Indians own multiple SIMs.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the three lines; mobile towers over the other two.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Mobile subscriptions count SIMs, not people, one person may have multiple.

chartExplainers.title: Mobile data used per person each month
chartExplainers.takeaway: Average monthly data use per subscriber exploded from 0.05 GB in 2013 to 23.85 GB in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart tracks how much data the average mobile subscriber consumed each quarter. In 2013, it was a tiny 0.05 GB, about 50 MB. The line stayed flat until 2016. Then it shot upward, hitting 23.85 GB by September 2025. The turning point was the entry of Reliance Jio in late 2016, which made high-speed data affordable. Today, India's per-subscriber data consumption is among the highest globally. This average includes light users, so heavy users consume much more.
chartExplainers.howToRead: See the sharp bend after 2016 when the line steepens.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is per subscriber, not per capita; many Indians still don't have smartphones.

chartExplainers.title: What a gigabyte of mobile data costs
chartExplainers.takeaway: The price of 1 GB of mobile data crashed from ₹200 in 2016 to ₹8 in 2025, a 96% drop.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the average price per gigabyte of mobile data in India, measured quarterly. In June 2016, a GB cost ₹200. Then the line plummets, reaching ₹8 by September 2025. The steepest drop happened in 2016-2017 after Jio launched free and cheap data. This price collapse is a visible pattern that correlates with the surge in data usage. When data became nearly free, people started streaming, video-calling, and using apps. But ₹8 is a national average; actual prices vary by plan and operator.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The chart is a mirror of the data usage chart: as price drops, usage climbs.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: ₹8/GB is an average; promo plans and pack sizes vary.

chartExplainers.title: UPI transactions each month
chartExplainers.takeaway: Monthly UPI transactions surged from 999.57 million in April 2020 to 22.35 billion in April 2026, a 22-fold increase.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the number of UPI transactions processed each month. In April 2020, it was just under 1 billion. The line rises steadily and rapidly, crossing 10 billion in early 2024 and reaching 22.35 billion by April 2026. UPI has become the backbone of digital payments in India, used for everything from street vendors to online shopping. The chart climbs at a near-constant rate month after month, showing no signs of slowing down.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Exponential curve, watch the consistent climb month over month.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Volume does not equal value; a ₹1 transaction counts the same as ₹10,000.

chartExplainers.title: Internet subscribers: wireless vs fixed line
chartExplainers.takeaway: Wireless internet subscribers (973.39 million) outnumber fixed line (44.42 million) by 22 times.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart compares the number of wireless and fixed-line internet subscribers in India from 2013 to 2025. The wireless line starts at 143.2 million in March 2013 and climbs to 973.39 million by September 2025. The fixed line starts at 21.61 million and rises slowly to 44.42 million. The gap is enormous: wireless is the way India goes online. Fixed line is for a tiny minority, mostly in cities and offices. The chart shows that India's internet is fundamentally mobile.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines: wireless is nearly 20 times larger than fixed.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Subscribers count active connections, not unique individuals.

chartExplainers.title: Rural vs urban telecom subscribers
chartExplainers.takeaway: Urban areas have 783.12 million telecom subscribers, rural only 554.41 million, but rural is catching up.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart compares total telecom subscribers (mobile + landline) in rural and urban India from 2011 to 2026. Both lines rise. In January 2011, urban had 538.38 million, rural had 267.74 million. By April 2026, urban reached 783.12 million, rural 554.41 million. The gap in absolute numbers has shrunk but remains. Rural subscribers more than doubled, while urban grew by less than 50%. So the divide is narrowing. But subscriber count does not measure speed or quality: rural connections may be slower.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Both lines rise, but urban stays higher; look at the slopes to see convergence.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Subscribers do not measure quality or speed; rural connections may be slower.

chartExplainers.title: Tele-density, total
chartExplainers.takeaway: TRAI put total tele-density at 84.76 in April 2026.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart uses TRAI monthly report PDFs to show telephone connections relative to population. Tele-density is helpful because it puts raw subscriber totals against a denominator. The April 2026 value is 84.76. That does not mean 84.76% of people personally own a phone, because one person can have multiple connections and some connections are machines or business lines. The chart should be read as network reach, not individual access.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Follow the line month by month, but read small moves with the methodology caveat in mind.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat tele-density as the share of unique people with phones.

chartExplainers.title: Mobile number portability requests
chartExplainers.takeaway: TRAI recorded 14.74 million MNP requests in April 2026.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart tracks monthly Mobile Number Portability requests from TRAI report PDFs. MNP is the system that lets a subscriber try to keep the same number while changing operators. The April 2026 number was 14.74 million requests. It adds a behaviour layer to the connectivity story: users are not only connected, they can also switch. But requests are not completed switches, and they are not unique people.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Read this as monthly switching pressure in the telecom market.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not count every request as one person changing operators.

chartExplainers.title: The gender gap online
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2025, 76.7% of men and 63.1% of women used the internet, a 13.6 percentage point gap.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart compares internet use by gender in 2018 and 2025. In 2018, 25% of men and 14.9% of women were online, a 10.1 point gap. By 2025, men's use rose to 76.7%, women's to 63.1%, a 13.6 point gap. The gap has widened in absolute percentage points, but both groups have improved dramatically. The proportion of women online quadrupled. Still, a woman in India is significantly less likely to be online than a man. The national average of 70% masks this inequality.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bar chart with two bars per year; focus on the difference between them.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is a national average; the gap is wider in rural and low-income groups.

chartExplainers.title: India vs China vs world, internet use
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's 70% internet use in 2025 is below China's 91.6% and the world average of 73.6%.
chartExplainers.detail: This bar chart compares the latest internet usage rates for India, China, and the world. In 2025, India stands at 70%, the world at 73.6%, and China at 91.6%. US data for 2025 is not available. India is slightly below the global average and significantly behind China. This puts India's progress in perspective: impressive from where it started, but there is room to improve. The comparison shows that other large countries have achieved higher penetration, often by investing in fixed broadband and digital literacy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bar chart showing the latest year; compare heights.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: US data is missing for 2025; don't assume India is 'ahead' of any country not shown.

sectionVisualMap.heading: How many Indians are online now?
What had to come before the internet boom?
Why did mobile beat fixed broadband?
How much data do Indians use now?
What happened to the price of data?
How far does the phone network reach?
What is the gender gap online?
How does India compare with the world?

sourceNotes: Internet-use share: Our World in Data grapher artifact for India.
Electricity, mobile subscriptions, fixed broadband, fixed telephone, and gender internet-use series: World Bank artifacts.
Mobile data use, mobile data cost, UPI transactions, and wireless/fixed internet subscribers: IndiaDataHub artifacts in the local repo.
World comparison uses the local World Bank world-context artifact; the United States has no 2025 value in that artifact and should not be shown for that year.

Rural/urban telecom subscribers, total telecom subscribers, tele-density, active wireless subscribers, broadband subscribers, and MNP requests: official TRAI monthly report PDFs extracted with native PDF text and Paddle structure fallback where needed.
caveats: Internet use is not the same as daily, fast, private, affordable, or meaningful access.
Mobile subscriptions count connections, not unique people. One person may hold more than one SIM.
UPI transaction count measures number of payments, not the value moved.
Rural and urban telecom subscriber counts are totals, not population-adjusted rates.
The gender chart proves a usage gap; it does not prove all causes of that gap.
TRAI subscriber counts are connections/subscriptions, not unique people. Tele-density is a ratio of connections to population, not personal ownership.

Wireless is internet through mobile towers, it goes to your phone. In India, most people use wireless because it's cheaper and easier to set up.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's internet is overwhelmingly wireless. That means coverage can be spotty in remote areas, and speeds may be lower than what fixed broadband offers.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The gap in internet access and use between people living in villages (rural) and those living in cities (urban). City dwellers usually have more reliable and faster connections because infrastructure is better.

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India has become highly connected, with 70% of its population online by 2025, driven by near-universal electricity, cheap mobile data at ₹8 per GB, and a predominantly wireless network that has reshaped payments and daily life.
requiredConcepts: Define the indicator: internet share is the percentage of people who have used the internet in the last three months.
Explain the unit: % of total population, not just adults or subscribers.
Explain the main chart: Indians online from 0% in 1990 to 70% in 2025.
Explain the most important supporting chart: electricity access rose from 50.9% to 99.5%, enabling connectivity.
State the most important caveat: national average hides large gaps by gender (men 76.7%, women 63.1%) and rural vs urban populations.

sectionPlan.heading: What share of Indians are now online?
sectionPlan.job: Show the headline: internet use climbed from 0% to 70% in 35 years.
lockedNumbersToUse: Individuals using the internet: Share of the population using the Internet
sectionPlan.caveat: The 70% figure is a model estimate; survey data may differ.

sectionPlan.heading: What made the internet boom possible?
sectionPlan.job: Explain that near-universal electricity (99.5%) was a prerequisite.
sectionPlan.caveat: Electricity access does not guarantee reliability; outages still affect connectivity.

sectionPlan.heading: Mobile, broadband, or landline: how does India connect?
sectionPlan.job: Show that mobile cellular subscriptions (79.35 per 100) dominate over fixed lines (3.15 broadband, 2.7 telephone).
sectionPlan.caveat: Subscriptions per 100 people count SIM cards, not people; multiple SIMs inflate the number.

sectionPlan.heading: How much mobile data does each subscriber use?
sectionPlan.job: Show the explosion from 0.05 GB in 2013 to 23.85 GB per month by late 2025.
lockedNumbersToUse: Mobile data used per subscriber per month, latest
Mobile data used per subscriber per month, earliest
sectionPlan.caveat: Averages hide variation; heavy users lift the mean.

sectionPlan.heading: What does a gigabyte of mobile data cost?
sectionPlan.job: Show the price collapse from ₹200 per GB in 2016 to ₹8 per GB in 2025.
sectionPlan.caveat: ₹8/GB is a national average; actual price varies by plan and operator.

sectionPlan.heading: How has connectivity changed the way India pays?
sectionPlan.job: Show UPI transactions growing from 999.57 million per month in 2020 to 22.35 billion per month in 2026.
sectionPlan.caveat: UPI volume does not capture value of transactions; also, cash remains widely used.

sectionPlan.heading: Is India's internet mostly wireless or fixed line?
sectionPlan.job: Show wireless subscribers at 973.39 million vs fixed line at 44.42 million.
sectionPlan.caveat: Subscribers count active connections, not unique users; one person may have multiple devices.

sectionPlan.heading: Where are the telecom connections: rural or urban?
sectionPlan.job: Show urban subscribers (783.12 million) still outnumber rural (554.41 million), but rural is catching up.
sectionPlan.caveat: Subscriber counts do not reflect data speed or quality; rural areas may have slower connections.

sectionPlan.heading: Is the gender gap in internet use narrowing?
sectionPlan.job: Show latest gap: men 76.7%, women 63.1%, a 13.6 percentage point gap, narrowed from 10.1 points in 2018.
sectionPlan.caveat: These are national averages; the gap is wider in rural areas and poorer households.

sectionPlan.heading: How does India's internet use compare to China and the world?
sectionPlan.job: Show India at 70%, world at 73.6%, China at 91.6%, India is slightly below the global average.
sectionPlan.caveat: China's data may include different measurement methods; US data not available for 2025.

chartPlan.title: Indians online, % of population
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The headline climb from 0% in 1990 to 70% in 2025, the most direct answer to the page question.
chartPlan.howToRead: Look at the steep rise after 2015; each number is the share of total population that used the internet.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse with 'subscribers', this counts actual users, not connections.

chartPlan.title: The foundation: access to electricity
chartPlan.whyShowThis: You need power to go online; electrification rose from 50.9% to 99.5% in 30 years, enabling the internet boom.
chartPlan.howToRead: Simple line from low to near 100%; watch for the long, slow rise before acceleration.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume reliable supply, access does not mean 24/7 electricity.

chartPlan.title: How India connects: mobile vs broadband vs landline
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India leapfrogged landlines: mobile subscriptions at 79 per 100 vs broadband at 3.15 and landline at 2.7.
chartPlan.howToRead: Compare the three lines; mobile towers over the other two.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Mobile subscriptions count SIMs, not people, one person may have multiple.

chartPlan.title: Mobile data used per person each month
chartPlan.whyShowThis: From 0.05 GB in 2013 to 23.85 GB in 2025, the Jio shock made data abundant.
chartPlan.howToRead: See the sharp bend after 2016 when the line steepens.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is per subscriber, not per capita; many Indians still don't have smartphones.

chartPlan.title: What a gigabyte of mobile data costs
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Price fell from ₹200/GB in 2016 to ₹8/GB in 2025, the driver of surging data use.
chartPlan.howToRead: The chart is a mirror of the data usage chart: as price drops, usage climbs.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: ₹8/GB is an average; promo plans and pack sizes vary.

chartPlan.title: UPI transactions each month
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Connectivity reshaped payments: from 999.57 million monthly transactions in 2020 to 22.35 billion in 2026.
chartPlan.howToRead: Exponential curve, watch the consistent climb month over month.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Volume does not equal value; a ₹1 transaction counts the same as ₹10,000.

chartPlan.title: Internet subscribers: wireless vs fixed line
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Wireless (973.39 million) dominates fixed line (44.42 million), India is mobile-first.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: wireless is nearly 20 times larger than fixed.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Subscribers count active connections, not unique individuals.

chartPlan.title: Rural vs urban telecom subscribers
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Urban has 783.12 million subscribers, rural 554.41 million, a persistent gap but rural is growing faster.
chartPlan.howToRead: Both lines rise, but urban stays higher; look at the slopes to see convergence.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Subscribers do not measure quality or speed; rural connections may be slower.

chartPlan.title: The gender gap online
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Men at 76.7%, women at 63.1%, a 13.6 point gap that has narrowed from 10.1 points in 2018.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bar chart with two bars per year; focus on the difference between them.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is a national average; the gap is wider in rural and low-income groups.

chartPlan.title: India vs China vs world, internet use
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India at 70% is below China (91.6%) and slightly below the world average (73.6%).
chartPlan.howToRead: Bar chart showing the latest year; compare heights.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: US data is missing for 2025; don't assume India is 'ahead' of any country not shown.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: Yes, the selected indicators cover the full story: level, prerequisite, composition, intensity, driver, payments, divides, and comparison.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Shows the long-term trend clearly on mobile
Connects prerequisite to internet growth
Allows comparison of three series on one chart
Shows the post-Jio spike with sufficient granularity
Mirror of data usage; quarterly shows the decline clearly
Monthly frequency captures rapid growth
Shows dominance of wireless over time
Monthly shows convergence trend

visualizationDecisions.bestChartType: latest bars (or small multiples for trend)
visualizationDecisions.reason: Easy to compare two bars for men and women

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: India vs China vs world internet use
visualizationDecisions.reason: Simple comparison across entities

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The percentage of people in a country who have used the internet in the past three months. It counts individuals, not devices.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It's the main measure of how connected a population is.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The amount of internet data consumed on a mobile phone network, usually measured in gigabytes (GB) per month per subscriber.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's massive usage is a sign of cheap data and deep mobile reliance.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A real-time payment system that lets you send money instantly between bank accounts using a smartphone app.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It shows how connectivity has transformed everyday transactions.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Fixed broadband is a wired connection to your home (like fiber or cable). Wireless uses mobile networks (3G/4G/5G) to connect devices.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's internet is overwhelmingly wireless, which explains coverage and speed differences.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The gap in access and usage of technology between people living in villages and those in cities.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Though telecom subscribers have grown in rural areas, urban areas still have far more connections.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The difference between men and women in access to and use of the internet and digital tools.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Men use the internet at significantly higher rates than women in India, though the gap is shrinking.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

evidence.question: How connected is India?

theme.label: Society, work, education, and services
readerQuestions: What basic capability is being measured?
How many people does the percentage represent?
Is this access, usage, quality, or outcome?
Which caveat stops the chart from being overclaimed?

plannedCharts.title: Indians online, % of population
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline climb from almost nobody to most of the country.

plannedCharts.title: The foundation: access to electricity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: You cannot connect what you cannot power; near-universal electricity came first.

plannedCharts.title: How India connects: mobile vs broadband vs landline
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · subscriptions per 100 people
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India leapfrogged wires and went straight to mobile.

plannedCharts.title: Mobile data used per person each month
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The Jio shock: data use exploded after 2016.

plannedCharts.title: What a gigabyte of mobile data costs
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The mirror image of the surge: price collapsed.

plannedCharts.title: UPI transactions each month
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Being connected reshaped how India pays.

plannedCharts.title: Internet subscribers: wireless vs fixed line
plannedCharts.subtitle: IndiaDataHub · TRAI · internet subscribers
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Almost all of India's internet rides on mobile networks.

plannedCharts.title: Rural vs urban telecom subscribers
plannedCharts.subtitle: TRAI monthly report PDFs · telephone subscribers
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The divide: where the connections actually are.

plannedCharts.title: Tele-density, total
plannedCharts.subtitle: TRAI monthly report PDFs · telephone connections per population
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Subscriber totals need a population lens; tele-density shows the rough reach of phone connections.

plannedCharts.title: Mobile number portability requests
plannedCharts.subtitle: TRAI monthly report PDFs · MNP requests
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A connected market is also a switchable market; MNP shows how many subscribers ask to move providers while keeping their number.

plannedCharts.title: The gender gap online
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · internet use by sex, latest available
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Connection is not shared equally between men and women.

plannedCharts.title: India vs China vs world, internet use
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: How India's connection rate stacks up globally.

geography.name: India, China, United States, and world

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
